Cleaned the prose: tightened sentences, removed redundancy, fixed punctuation/dash usage, smoothed transitions, and made the empirical claims read more crisply. Substantive content and structure preserved.

---

Molecular property prediction over multi-source bioactivity data suffers from a subtle form of label ambiguity. A single "IC50" entry may denote enzymatic inhibition, cell-based potency, or a biophysical binding readout, each governed by distinct physics and spanning substantially different dynamic ranges. Naive aggregation across these sources—standard practice with public bioactivity warehouses—collapses physically distinct quantities into a single regression target, with predictable degradation in downstream model fidelity. We introduce AssayAware, a transformer encoder over SMILES that augments standard tokenization with a learned assay-context vector, allowing the model to disambiguate label semantics during multi-task fine-tuning across heterogeneous bioactivity records.

The principal technical contribution is the assay-conditioning mechanism itself. We show that, under a mild observability condition on the assay-context distribution—each assay class is paired with at least three pharmacophorically distinct ligand families—the model recovers per-assay calibration shifts as a byproduct of multi-task training. This yields a principled route for transferring across assay families, which earlier multi-task chemical models handled only through hand-curated unit conversions or by silently discarding inconsistent records.

The AssayAware encoder is a 12-layer transformer with 768-dimensional hidden states and 12 attention heads, tokenizing SMILES with a BPE vocabulary of 8,192 merges. We pretrain on 5M canonicalized SMILES from ChEMBL release 33 with a masked-token objective at a 15% mask rate, optimized with AdamW (peak learning rate 2e-4, linear warmup over 6,000 steps, batch size 256). Fine-tuning uses a 12-task subset spanning kinase, GPCR, and ion-channel targets, with a Bemis–Murcko scaffold split at 80/10/10.

Empirically, assay conditioning reduces per-task RMSE by 0.18–0.31 log-units relative to an identical encoder trained without conditioning. More strikingly, it recovers a meaningful calibration manifold in the learned assay-embedding space, with cell-based and biochemical assays separating along a single dominant axis. This separation is consistent with the theoretical prediction that conditioning performs identifiable disambiguation rather than merely adding a per-task bias, and suggests the assay embeddings may themselves serve as a diagnostic tool for auditing the aggregation pipelines behind public bioactivity benchmarks.
